questions.explanation: Old is a short adjective, so the comparative adds -er: older than. "old" is the base form (no comparison), "more old" wrongly puts more on a short adjective, and "oldest" is the superlative (and would need the).

questions.prompt: Complete: "A train is ___ than a bicycle."
questions.explanation: Fast is short, so the comparative is faster than. "more fast" doubles up on a short adjective, "fast" is the plain base form, and "fastest" is the superlative form, which needs the and doesn't take than.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This box is ___ than that one."
questions.explanation: Heavy ends in consonant + y, so the y becomes i before -er: heavier than. "heavyer" keeps the y wrongly, "more heavy" treats a two-syllable -y adjective as long (it isn't), and "heaviest" is the superlative.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Maths is ___ than English for me."
questions.explanation: Difficult is a long adjective (three syllables), so the comparative is more difficult than. "difficulter" wrongly adds -er to a long adjective, "more difficulter" double-marks it, and "most difficult" is the superlative, which can't follow than.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The weather today is ___ than yesterday."
questions.explanation: Bad is irregular: its comparative is worse (worse than yesterday). "more bad" and "badder" both invent a regular form that doesn't exist, and "worst" is the superlative (the worst), which doesn't take than.

options.text: She is more taller than her sister.
She is more tall than her sister.
She is taller than her sister.
She is taller then her sister.

questions.explanation: Tall is short, so the comparative is taller, and a comparison uses than: taller than her sister. "more taller" double-marks the comparison, "more tall" misuses more on a short adjective, and "then" is a time word, not the comparison word than.
